audit_status.source_base: Hebrew 2 Samuel chapter 21, first-pass draft aligned later
audit_status.review_note: Reviewed for famine theology, Saul's attempted destruction of Gibeonites, treaty obligations, descendant punishment, David's selection of seven men, Mephibosheth's exemption, Michal/Merab textual issue, execution and corpse exposure, Rizpah's vigil, delayed burial, aging and battlefield fatigue, giant traditions, Goliath contradiction, and polydactyly.

verses.restored: There was famine in days of David, three years, year after year. David sought face of YHWH. YHWH said, It is because of Saul and because of house of bloodguilt, because he put Gibeonites to death.

King called Gibeonites and spoke to them. Gibeonites were not from children of Israel but from remnant of Amorites. Children of Israel had sworn to them, but Saul sought to strike them in his zeal for children of Israel and Judah.
David said to Gibeonites, What shall I do for you, and with what shall I make atonement, that you may bless inheritance of YHWH?
Gibeonites said to him, There is no silver or gold for us with Saul and his house, and it is not for us to put a man to death in Israel. He said, What you say, I will do for you.
They said to king, The man who consumed us and planned against us, that we should be destroyed from standing within any territory of Israel—
let seven men from his sons/descendants be given to us, and we will hang/expose them before YHWH at Gibeah of Saul, chosen of YHWH. King said, I will give them.
King spared Mephibosheth son of Jonathan son of Saul because of oath of YHWH between them, between David and Jonathan son of Saul.

King took two sons of Rizpah daughter of Aiah whom she bore to Saul, Armoni and Mephibosheth, and five sons of Michal/Merab daughter of Saul whom she bore/raised for Adriel son of Barzillai the Meholathite.
He gave them into hand of Gibeonites. They hanged/exposed them on mountain before YHWH, and the seven fell together. They were put to death in first days of harvest, at beginning of barley harvest.

Rizpah daughter of Aiah took sackcloth and spread it for herself upon rock, from beginning of harvest until water poured upon them from heavens. She did not allow birds of heavens to rest upon them by day or animals of field by night.
It was told David what Rizpah daughter of Aiah, concubine of Saul, had done.

David went and took bones of Saul and bones of Jonathan his son from leaders of Jabesh-gilead, who had stolen them from square of Beth-shan where Philistines had hung them on day Philistines struck Saul at Gilboa.
He brought up from there bones of Saul and bones of Jonathan his son, and they gathered bones of those who had been hanged/exposed.
They buried bones of Saul and Jonathan his son in land of Benjamin at Zela, in grave of Kish his father. They did all king commanded. After this Divine was entreated for land.
Philistines again had war with Israel. David went down, and his servants with him, and they fought Philistines. David became weary.
Ishbi-benob, who was among descendants of Rapha, whose spear weighed three hundred bronze shekels and who was girded with a new sword, intended to strike David.

Abishai son of Zeruiah rescued him, struck Philistine, and put him to death. Then men of David swore to him, saying, You shall not go out with us again to battle, so you do not extinguish lamp of Israel.
After this there was again battle with Philistines at Gob. Then Sibbecai the Hushathite struck Saph, who was among descendants of Rapha.
There was again battle with Philistines at Gob. Elhanan son of Jaare-oregim the Bethlehemite struck Goliath the Gittite, shaft of whose spear was like beam of weavers.
There was again battle at Gath. There was a man of great size who had six fingers on each hand and six toes on each foot, twenty-four in number; he also was born to Rapha.
He taunted Israel, and Jonathan son of Shimei, brother of David, struck him.
These four were born to Rapha in Gath, and they fell by hand of David and by hand of his servants.

translation_notes: The famine is attributed to Saul's bloodguilt against Gibeonites, preserving memory of an attempted treaty-breaking destruction. Modern famine, drought, crop failure, or food insecurity must never be diagnosed as punishment for hidden sin.

Gibeonites are a protected remnant whose covenant was violated. Their survival and grievance matter, but the killing of seven descendants who did not commit Saul's acts is collective punishment, not ethical atonement.
David chooses which descendants live and die, sparing Jonathan's son under one covenant while handing over others. Selective oath loyalty does not make the exchange just.

Verse 8 has a major Michal/Merab textual problem. Michal is elsewhere said to have no child; many witnesses and interpreters read Merab. The women and sons must not be treated as interchangeable dynastic inventory.
Rizpah's months-long vigil is embodied protest against execution, exposure, scavenging, and royal neglect. She compels belated burial without receiving direct speech from the king.

The claim that Divine was entreated after burial reports the narrative's theology; it must not imply that killing descendants caused rain or that sacrifice of vulnerable people repairs state violence.
David's weariness is not shameful weakness. His soldiers establish a safety boundary around an aging or exhausted leader.
The Goliath verse conflicts with 1 Samuel 17 and 1 Chronicles 20, which names Lahmi, Goliath's brother. Source seams must remain visible.
Six fingers and six toes are congenital bodily variation, not monstrosity, evil, or proof of nonhuman identity.
The repeated 'giant' battles are warrior traditions and may not support ethnic hatred, ableism, body-based moral judgment, or glorification of killing.
safety_note: Maximum safeguards: Do not use famine to blame communities, execute descendants for ancestors, or call exposed bodies an acceptable payment to Divine. Center the Gibeonites' violated treaty and Rizpah's protest while refusing collective punishment. Do not stigmatize aging, fatigue, unusual height, polydactyly, or other bodily difference.
